sections.heading: Chapter 4: Medication management and drug stewardship in CKD

children.heading: Practice Point 4.1.1: People with CKD may be more sus\- cepible to the nephrotoxic effects of medications. When prescribing such medications to people with CKD, always consider the benefits versus potential harms.
4.1 Medication choices and monitoring for safety
4.2 Dose adjustments by level of eGFR
Practice Point 4.2.1: Consider eGFR when dosing medications cleared by the kidneys.
Practice Point 4.2.2: For most people and clinical settings, validated eGFR equations using SCr are appropriate for drug dosing.
Practice Point 4.2.5: Consider and adapt drug dosing in people where eGFR, non-eGFR determinants of the filtration markers, or volume of distribution are not in a steady state.
4.3 Polypharmacy and drug stewardship

Practice Point 4.3.2: If medications are discontinued during an acute illness, communicate a clear plan of when to restart the discontinued medications to the affected person and healthcare providers, and ensure documentation in the medical record.
4.3.1 Strategies to promote drug stewardship
4.4 Imaging studies
4.4.1 Radiocontrast: intra-arterial and intravenous dye studies
Practice Point 4.4.1.1: Assess the risk for AKI in people with CKD receiving intra-arterial contrast for cardiac procedures using validated tools.
4.4.2 Gadolinium-containing contrast media

Practice Point 4.4.1.2: The intravenous administration of radiocontrast media can be managed in accordance with consensus statements from the radiology societies in people with AKI or GFR <60 mL/min/1.73m² (CKD G3a–G5) undergoing elective investigation.

Practice Point 4.4.2.1: For people with GFR <30 mL/min/1.73m² (CKD G4–G5) who require gadolinium\-containing contrast media, preferentially offer them American College of Radiology group II and III gadolinium\-based contrast agents.

sections.heading: For research recommendations, please see Chapter 6: Research recommendations.

children.heading: 3.3.2 Sodium intake
Recommendation 3.3.2.1: We suggest that sodium intake be <2 g of sodium per day (or <90 mmol of sodium per day, or <5 g of sodium chloride per day) in people with CKD (2C).
3.4 Blood pressure control
Recommendation 3.4.1: We suggest that adults with high BP and CKD be treated with a target systolic blood pressure (SBP) of <120 mm Hg, when tolerated, using standardized office BP measurement (2B).

Recommendation 3.4.2: We suggest that in children with CKD, 24-hour mean arterial pressure (MAP) by ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) should be lowered to ≤50th percentile for age, sex, and height (2C).
3.5 Glycemic control
3.6 Renin-angiotensin system inhibitors
Practice Point 3.6.7: Continue ACEi or ARB in people with CKD even when the eGFR falls below 30 ml/min per 1.73\ \mathrm{m}^{2}.
3.7 Sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors (SGLT2i)
Recommendation 3.7.1: We recommend treating patients with type 2 diabetes (T2D), CKD, and an eGFR ≥20 mL/min/1.73m² with an SGLT2i (1A).
Key information
3.11.1 Awareness of factors impacting on potassium measurement

Practice Point 3.11.1.1: Be aware of the variability of potassium laboratory measurements as well as factors and mechanisms that may influence potassium measurement including diurnal and seasonal variation, plasma versus serum samples, and the actions of medications.
3.11.2 Potassium exchange agents
Practice Point 3.11.2.1: Be aware of local availability or formulary restrictions with regard to the pharmacologic management of nonemergent hyperkalemia.
3.11.3 Timing to recheck potassium after identifying moderate and severe hyperkalemia in adults
3.11.4 Managing hyperkalemia
3.11.5 Dietary considerations
Practice Point 3.15.1.4: Consider prescribing proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9 (PCSK-9) inhibitors to people with CKD who have an indication for their use.
Practice Point 3.15.1.5: Consider a plant\-based “Mediter\- ranean\-style” diet in addition to lipid\-modifying therapy to reduce cardiovascular risk.
3.15.2 Use of antiplatelet therapy

Recommendation 3.15.2.1: We recommend oral low-dose aspirin for prevention of recurrent ischemic cardiovascular disease events (i.e., secondary prevention) in people with CKD and established ischemic cardiovascular disease (1C).
Rationale
Practice Point 3.15.2.1: Consider other antiplatelet therapy (e.g., P2Y_{12} inhibitors) when there is aspirin intolerance.
Key information
3.15.3 Invasive versus intensive medical therapy for coronary artery disease

Recommendation 3.15.3.1: We suggest that in stable stress-test confirmed ischemic heart disease, an initial conservative approach using intensive medical therapy is an appropriate alternative to an initial invasive strategy (2D).
Rationale
3.16 CKD and atrial fibrillation
Practice Point 3.16.1: Follow established strategies for the diagnosis and management of atrial fibrillation (Figure 40).
Key information

Recommendation 3.16.1: We recommend use of non-vitamin K antagonist oral anticoagulants (NOACs) in preference to vitamin K antagonists (e.g., warfarin) for thromboprophylaxis in atrial fibrillation in people with CKD G1–G4 (1C).
Rationale
Practice Point 3.16.2: NOAC dose adjustment for eGFR is required, with caution needed at CKD G4–G5.
Key information
1.1 Detection and evaluation of CKD
1.1.1 Detection of CKD
Practice Point 1.1.1.2: Following incidental detection of elevated urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio (ACR), hematuria, or low estimated eGFR (eGFR), repeat tests to confirm presence of CKD.
Key information
1.1.2 Methods for staging of CKD

Recommendation 1.1.2.1: In adults at risk for CKD, we recommend using creatinine-based estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFRcr). If cystatin C is available, the eGFR category should be estimated from the combination of creatinine and cystatin C (creatinine and cystatin C–based estimated glomerular filtration rate [eGFRcr-cys]) (1B).
1.1.3 Evaluation of chronicity
Rationale
1.1.4 Evaluation of cause
Recommendation 1.1.4.1: We suggest performing a kidney biopsy as an acceptable, safe, diagnostic test to evaluate cause and guide treatment decisions when clinically appropriate (2D).
Key information

tables.otsl_text: <ched>Nephrotoxic medication</ched><ched>Potential non-nephrotoxic alternatives</ched><nl><fcel>Analgesics</fcel><fcel>Acetaminophen</fcel><nl><fcel>NSAIDs: nephrotoxic effects include a decrease in GFR through a reduction in prostaglandin-dependent kidney blood flow, allergic interstitial nephritis (AIN), and nephrotic syndrome 725</fcel><fcel></fcel><nl><fcel>Antimicrobials</fcel><fcel>Cephalosporins and carbapenems</fcel><nl><fcel>Aminoglycosides: accumulates in the proximal tubular cells and disrupts phospholipid metabolism, resulting in cell apoptosis and acute tubular necrosis (ATN 731,732</fcel><fcel>Linezolid and dapomycin 731</fcel><nl><fcel>Vancomycin: unclear cause of nephrotoxicity, but likely related to ATN and possible AIN 731,732</fcel><fcel>Clindamycin þ primaquine, pentamidine, and atovaquone</fcel><nl><fcel>Sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim: AIN, ATN, crystalluria within the distal convoluted tubule and reversible inhibition of tubular creatinine secretion 731</fcel><fcel></fcel><nl><fcel>Gastrointestinal medications</fcel><fcel>H2-receptor antagonists</fcel><nl><fcel>Proton pump inhibitors: may result in AKI and CKD due to tubulointerstitial nephritis and AIN 733,734</fcel><fcel></fcel><nl><fcel>Cardiovascular medications</fcel><fcel>Non-vitamin K antagonist oral anticoagulants</fcel><nl><fcel>Warfarin: glomerular hemorrhage, oxidative stress causing kidney tubular damage, and direct effects on kidney vascular calcification by vitamin K-dependent alterations of matrix Gla protein 735,736</fcel><fcel></fcel><nl><fcel>Other</fcel><fcel>Aripiprazole, lamotrigine, quetiapine, valproate</fcel><nl><fcel>Lithium: nephrogenic diabetes insipidus as well as CKD from chronic tubulointerstitial nephropathy 737</fcel><fcel></fcel><nl><fcel>CKD, chronic kidney disease; GFR, glomerular filtration rate; NSAID, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug.</fcel><fcel></fcel><nl><fcel>Kidney disease can be induced or accelerated by the use of certain over-the-counter (OTC) medications, herbal remedies, and other dietary supplements. One of the most used class of OTC analgesic medications is NSAIDs. NSAIDs are associated with interstitial nephritis, analgesic nephropathy, and hypertension. 739 Indiscriminate chronic OTC NSAID use has been associated with a higher risks of kidney failure compared with nonuse and should be discouraged. 740-743 However, judicious NSAID use, under careful supervision of a nephrologist, may be preferred to other pain medications such as opioids that have stronger associations with adverse events. 744,745 PPIs are also common OTC medications in some countries that have been associated with AKI and CKD due to tubulointerstitial nephritis and acute interstitial nephritis. 733,734</fcel><fcel></fcel>

<ched>Class</ched><ched>Mechanism</ched><ched>Example</ched><nl><fcel>ACEi</fcel><fcel>Inhibit conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II</fcel><fcel>Captopril, lisinopril, perindopril, etc.</fcel><nl><fcel>ARB</fcel><fcel>Inhibit activation of angiotensin I receptor by angiotensin II</fcel><fcel>Losartan, irbesartan, candesartan, etc.</fcel><nl><fcel>Aldosterone antagonist</fcel><fcel>Block aldosterone receptor activation</fcel><fcel>Spironolactone, eplerenone, and finerenone</fcel><nl><fcel>β -Adrenergic receptor blocker</fcel><fcel>Inhibit renin release</fcel><fcel>Propranolol, metoprolol, and atenolol</fcel><nl><fcel>Digitalis glycoside</fcel><fcel>Inhibit Na $^{+}$-K $^{+}$-ATPase, necessary for collecting duct K + secretion</fcel><fcel>Digoxin</fcel><nl><fcel>Heparin</fcel><fcel>Reduced production of aldosterone</fcel><fcel>Heparin sodium</fcel><nl><fcel>Potassium-sparing diuretic</fcel><fcel>Block collecting duct apical Na + channel, decreasing gradient for K + secretion</fcel><fcel>Amiloride and triamterene</fcel><nl><fcel>NSAIDs</fcel><fcel>Inhibit synthesis of prostaglandin E and prostacyclin, inhibiting renin release</fcel><fcel>Ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenac, etc.</fcel><nl><fcel>CNI</fcel><fcel>Inhibit Na $^{+}$-K $^{+}$-ATPase, necessary for collecting duct K + secretion</fcel><fcel>Cyclosporine and tacrolimus</fcel><nl><fcel>ns-MRA</fcel><fcel>Block MR-mediated Na + reabsorption</fcel><fcel>Finerone</fcel><nl><fcel>Other</fcel><fcel>Block collecting duct apical Na + channel, decreasing gradient for K + secretion</fcel><fcel>Trimethoprim and pentamidine</fcel>

<ched></ched><ched>(Polystyrene sulfonates) sodium or calcium</ched><ched>(Polystyrene sulfonates) sodium or calcium</ched><ched>Patiromer</ched><ched>Sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (SZC)</ched><nl><fcel>Mechanism of action</fcel><fcel>Sodium-potassium exchange resin (SPS) or calcium-potassium exchange resin (CPS)</fcel><fcel>Calcium-potassium exchange polymer</fcel><fcel>Crystalline compound that traps K + in exchange for hydrogen and sodium cations</fcel><fcel></fcel><nl><fcel>Counterion content</fcel><fcel>SPS: Suspension contains 65 mmol/60 ml (15 g) of sodium and powder approximately 4.1 mmol/g of sodium. CPS: 1.6-24 mmol/g of calcium.</fcel><fcel>1600 mg of calcium per 8.4 g of pairotimer</fcel><fcel>Approximately 400 mg of sodium per 5 g of SZC</fcel><fcel></fcel><nl><fcel>Cations bound</fcel><fcel>Potassium, magnesium, and calcium</fcel><fcel>Potassium, magnesium, and phosphate (bound by calcium release) 582</fcel><fcel>Potassium</fcel><fcel></fcel><nl><fcel>Formulation of route of administration</fcel><fcel>Powder for reconstitution (oral), suspension (oral), and enema (rectal)</fcel><fcel>Powder for reconstitution (oral)</fcel><fcel>Powder for reconstitution (oral suspension)</fcel><fcel></fcel><nl><fcel>Dosage and titration</fcel><fcel>Oral: 15-60 g/d (up to 4 times per day) Rectal: 30 g/d (for SPS up to a maximum of 50 g/d)</fcel><fcel>Initial: 8.4 g orally once per day (maximum 25.2 g orally once per day); dose can be increased by 8.4 g increments at 1-week intervals</fcel><fcel>Initial: 10 g orally 3 times per day for up to 48 hours</fcel><fcel></fcel><nl><fcel>Maintenance dosing</fcel><fcel>15-60 g/d orally per day depending on potassium level and level of tolerability</fcel><fcel>8.4-25.2 g orally once per day</fcel><fcel>5 g every second day to 10 g once per day</fcel><fcel></fcel><nl><fcel>Onset of effect</fcel><fcel>Variable, hours to days</fcel><fcel>4-7 hours</fcel><fcel>Starts to reduce potassium within 1 hour with normokalemia typically at 24-48 hours</fcel><fcel></fcel><nl><fcel>Duration of effect</fcel><fcel>Variable, 6-24 hours</fcel><fcel>24 hours</fcel><fcel>Not studied; not systematically absorbed and excreted fecally</fcel><fcel></fcel><nl><fcel>Administration pearls</fcel><fcel>Separate from oral medications by at least 3 hours before or 3 hours after administration; if gastroparesis, separate other medications by 6 hours</fcel><fcel>Separate from oral medications by at least 3 hours before or 3 hours after administration except for those drugs to not have a clinically important interaction</fcel><fcel>No dose adjustments or separation of time of dosing is required for any medication that does not have pHdependent bioavailability. However, SZC should be administered at least 2 hours before or 2 hours after oral medicinal products with clinically meaningful gastric pH-dependent bioavailability</fcel><fcel></fcel><nl><fcel>Adverse effects</fcel><fcel>GI events (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation), electrolyte disturbances (hypokalemia, hypocalcemia, and hypomagnesemia), edema, and potentially serious GI adverse events (intestinal necrosis, bleeding, ischemic colitis, and perforation)</fcel><fcel>GI events (nausea, diarrhea, and flatulence), electrolyte disturbances (hypokalemia, hypercalcemia, and hypomagnesemia) Insufficient postmarketing surveillance at present to evaluate long-term/ rare events</fcel><fcel>Hypokalemia and edema events are the most common. Milder reports of GI events (nausea, diarrhea, and constipation) Insufficient postmarketing surveillance at present to evaluate long-term/ rare events</fcel><fcel></fcel>

<ched>Severity of hyperkalemia</ched><ched>Clinically unwell or AKI</ched><ched>Unexpected result</ched><nl><fcel>Moderate K + 6.0-6.4 mmol/l</fcel><fcel>Assess and treat in hospital</fcel><fcel>Repeat within 24 hours</fcel><nl><fcel>Severe K + $6.5 mmol/l</fcel><fcel>Take immediate action to assess and treat. Assessment will include blood testing and electrocardiogram monitoring</fcel><fcel></fcel>

<ched>Clinical decisions</ched><ched>Current level</ched><ched>Current level</ched><ched>Current level</ched><nl><fcel>Diagnosis and staging</fcel><fcel>Detection of CKD</fcel><fcel>Albuminuria</fcel><fcel>Change in the level of GFR</fcel><nl><fcel>Treatment</fcel><fcel>· Evaluation for kidney donation · Referral to nephrologists · Patient and family education about CKD and benefit of lifestyle changes · Monitor progression of GFR decline · Referral for kidney transplantation · Placement of dialysis access · Dosage and monitoring for medications cleared by the kidney</fcel><fcel>· Detection of CKD · Referral to nephrologists · Patient and family education about CKD and benefit of lifestyle changes · Monitor progression of GFR decline · Eligibility for clinical trials</fcel><fcel>· Detection of AKI and AKD · Detection of CKD progression · Treatment of AKI · Monitoring drug toxicity · Re-evaluate CKD treatment strategies</fcel><nl><fcel>Risk assessment</fcel><fcel>Risk of CKD complications · Risk for CKD progression · Risk of CVD · Risk for medication errors · Risk for perioperative complications · Risk for mortality · Fertility and risk of complications of pregnancy</fcel><fcel>Risk for CKD progression · Risk for CVD · Risk for mortality · Fertility and risk of complications of pregnancy</fcel><fcel>Risk for kidney failure · Risk for CVD, HF, and mortality · Risk for adverse pregnancy outcome</fcel>

<ched>Domains</ched><ched>Example conditions</ched><nl><fcel>Common risk factors</fcel><fcel>Hypertension Diabetes Cardiovascular disease (including heart failure) Prior AKI/AKD</fcel><nl><fcel>People who live in geographical areas with high prevalence of CKD</fcel><fcel>Areas with endemic CKDu Areas with the high prevalence of APOL1 genetic variants Environmental exposures</fcel><nl><fcel>Genitourinary disorders</fcel><fcel>Structural urinary tract disease Recurrent kidney calculi</fcel><nl><fcel>Multisystem diseases/chronic inflammatory conditions</fcel><fcel>Systemic lupus erythematosus Vasculitis HIV</fcel><nl><fcel>Iatrogenic (related to drug treatments and procedures)</fcel><fcel>Drug-induced nephrotoxicity and radiation nephritis</fcel><nl><fcel>Family history or known genetic variant associated with CKD</fcel><fcel>Kidney failure, regardless of identified cause Kidney disease recognized to be associated with genetic abnormality (e.g., PKD, APOL1 -mediated kidney disease, and Alport syndrome)</fcel><nl><fcel>Gestational conditions</fcel><fcel>Preterm birth Small gestational size Pre-eclampsia/eclampsia</fcel><nl><fcel>Occupational exposures that promote CKD risk</fcel><fcel>Cadmium, lead, and mercury exposure Polycyclic hydrocarbons Pesticides</fcel>

<ched>Test category</ched><ched>Examples</ched><ched>Comment or key references</ched><nl><fcel>Imaging</fcel><fcel>Ultrasound, intravenous urography, CT kidneys ureters bladder, nuclear medicine studies, MRI</fcel><fcel>Assess kidney structure (i.e., kidney shape, size, symmetry, and evidence of obstruction) for cystic disease and reflux disease. Evolving role of additional technologies (e.g., 3D ultrasound)</fcel><nl><fcel>Kidney biopsy</fcel><fcel>Ultrasound-guided percutaneous</fcel><fcel>Usually examined by light microscopy, immunofluorescence, and electron microscopy, and, in some situations, may include molecular diagnostics Used for exact diagnosis, planning treatment, assessing activity and chronicity of disease, and likelihood of treatment response; may also be used to assess genetic disease</fcel><nl><fcel>Laboratory tests: serologic, urine tests</fcel><fcel>Chemistry including acid-base and electrolytes, serologic tests such as anti-PLA2R, ANCA, anti-GBM antibodies Serum-free light chains, serum, and urine protein electrophoresis/immunofixation Urinalysis and urine sediment examination</fcel><fcel>Refer to KDIGO 2021 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Glomerular Diseases 22 Increasing recognition of the role of light chains in kidney disease even in the absence of multiple myeloma (monoclonal gammopathy of renal significance [MGRS]) 98 Presence of persistent hematuria or albuminuria is critical in determining differential diagnosis</fcel><nl><fcel>Genetic testing</fcel><fcel>APOL1, COL4A3, COL4A4, COL4A5, NPHS1, UMOD, HNF1B, PKD1, PKD2</fcel><fcel>Evolving as a tool for diagnosis, increased utilization is expected. Recognition that genetic causes are more common and may present without classic family history 99,100</fcel>

<ched>GFR assessment method</ched><ched>Specific tests</ched><ched>Guidance for use and implementation</ched><nl><fcel>Estimated GFR</fcel><fcel>Creatinine (eGFRcr)</fcel><fcel>Most used method to assess GFR. In most cases, initial test for the evaluation of GFR. Standardized assay required to decrease between-center analytical variation</fcel><nl><fcel></fcel><fcel>Cystatin C (eGFRcr-cys, eGFRcys)</fcel><fcel>Used in selected circumstances as listed in Table 8 Standardized assay required to decrease between-center analytical variation</fcel><nl><fcel>mGFR</fcel><fcel>Gold standard. Urinary or plasma clearance of exogenous markers (e.g., iohexol, iothalamate,  $^{51}$Cr-EDTA, and 99mTc-DTPA)</fcel><fcel>Used in selected circumstances as listed in Table 8 Standard protocols for clearance methods and for the standardized assay</fcel><nl><fcel>Timed urine clearance</fcel><fcel>Creatinine</fcel><fcel>Highly prone to errors and recommended only when no other options for supportive tests for GFR evaluation; performance under supervised conditions may decrease error</fcel><nl><fcel>Nuclear medicine imaging</fcel><fcel>Imaging of the kidneys after injection of tracer cleared by the kidneys (e.g., 99mTc-DTPA scintigraphy)</fcel><fcel>Highly prone to errors; not recommended</fcel>

headers: Estimated GFR by SCr and/or cystatin C
tables.otsl_text: <ched>Estimated GFR by SCr and/or cystatin C</ched><ched>Measured GFR</ched><nl><fcel>Inexpensive and easy to implement</fcel><fcel>More expensive, more time-consuming, and invasive</fcel><nl><fcel>Widely available and may also be used at point of care, easily repeatable</fcel><fcel>Only available in certain centers Methods to measure that do not require urine collections are available (i.e., plasma clearance) Most protocols require repeat blood samples potentially over a long duration Microsampling tests by fingerpick enable point-of-care testing. Testing has been described, but not routinely performed</fcel><nl><fcel>Not sufficiently accurate and precise for all clinical situations</fcel><fcel>Accurate for GFR in all situations and across the GFR range. Requires individualized protocols</fcel><nl><fcel>Lags behind changes in GFR</fcel><fcel>Able to identify early changes in GFR</fcel><nl><fcel>Subject to non-GFR determinant confounding</fcel><fcel>Less influenced by non-GFR determinants</fcel><nl><fcel>GFR, glomerular filtration rate; SCr, serum creatinine.</fcel><fcel></fcel>
